text: Internationalization in Higher Education: Trends and Issues This paper gives an account of the growing internationalization of higher education from two main perspectives: globalizing forces and reactions of individual institutions. After a summary of current debates on this theme, the paper reflects on the underlying motivation from a political and cultural to a market-oriented direction. Developments in different regions of the world are differentiated. The scientifically and technologically leading Western superpowers represent one extreme, while developing countries, such as India, have to contend with the brain drain and entry of foreign educational companies. The diversity of the national funding patterns of higher education and the diversity of the forms of transnational education, including the use of campuses abroad and learning at a distance are also analysed. While internationalization of higher education is in general popular with the American public, the United States seems to be far from having a properly coordinated national policy on the matter. As compared with, for example, the EU's and the Commonwealth's purposes in this regard, the USA seems to be still waiting for a realistic occasion to participate more effectively and purposefully in the global higher education community.
